Adaptive Neural Network for CO2 Reduction: 
A Predictive Model Integrating MCMC 
and GAT Techniques for Oil Vessel Supply Chain 
Lapo Chirici1envelope symbol, Yi Wang2, and Kesheng Wang3 
1 Department of Computer Science, Università Di Pisa, Pisa, Italy 
lapochirici@gmail.com 
2 Business School, University of Bedfordshire, Luton, UK 
yi.wang@beds.ac.uk 
3 Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering, NTNU, Trondheim, Norway 
kesheng.wang@ntnu.no 
Abstract. This paper proposes an innovative machine learning algorithm that 
integrates Graph Attention Networks (GAT) with Monte Carlo Markov Chain 
(MCMC) techniques to optimize the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) in crude 
oil vessel production. This novel approach aims to reduce CO2 emissions, min-
imize lead times, and enhance cost savings within the supply chain. A synthetic 
dataset representing 15,000 companies in the oil and gas sector was used to test the 
algorithm. The results demonstrate potential improvements in key metrics, paving 
the way for more sustainable and efﬁcient supply chain operations. 
Keywords: Graph Theory cdotMCMC cdotNeural Network cdotGAT cdotCarbon Footprint 
1 
Introduction 
The crude oil industry, particularly in the production of vessels, faces signiﬁcant pres-
sures to balance environmental sustainability with operational efﬁciency. The Interna-
tional Energy Agency (IEA) reports that maritime activities contribute approximately 
2.5% of global CO2 emissions, with crude oil vessels representing a substantial portion 
of this impact. As environmental regulations become more stringent, the industry must 
adopt innovative strategies to minimize its carbon footprint while maintaining compet-
itive advantage. Current trends in the sector indicate a growing emphasis on integrating 
advanced technologies into supply chain operations to achieve these goals. For instance, 
there has been a signiﬁcant increase in the adoption of digital technologies and AI-driven 
solutions to enhance operational efﬁciency and reduce emissions. According to recent 
industry analyses, the global maritime industry’s move towards greener practices is 
expected to drive substantial investments in sustainable technologies, potentially reach-
ing $1 trillion by 2030. This shift is partly driven by international regulatory frameworks, 
such as the IMO 2020 sulphur cap and the European Union’s Emissions Trading System 
(ETS), which are pushing the industry towards lower emissions. This paper introduces

a novel approach to optimizing Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) conﬁgurations in 
crude oil vessel production by integrating Graph Attention Networks (GAT) and Monte 
Carlo Markov Chain (MCMC) techniques. The proposed methodology aims to reduce 
CO2 emissions and enhance overall supply chain performance by dynamically prioritiz-
ing key processes and materials. The integration of GATs, which are adept at managing 
the complexity of graph-structured data, with MCMC methods, known for their robust-
ness in probabilistic simulations, offers a comprehensive and innovative solution for 
addressing the environmental and operational challenges faced by the crude oil vessel 
production industry. 
2 
Machine Learning in Oil and Gas: Historical Prospective 
Machine Learning (ML) has revolutionized demand forecasting in the oil and gas sector, 
enabling companies to predict future demand with unprecedented accuracy by analysing 
historical data, market trends, and other relevant factors. ML algorithms, such as Ran-
dom Forests, Gradient Boosting Machines, and Neural Networks, can capture complex 
relationships in data, offering more accurate predictions than traditional methods. These 
advancements have led to signiﬁcant cost reductions and improved operational efﬁciency 
for companies like Shell, ExxonMobil, and Chevron, which have implemented ML for 
enhanced demand forecasting accuracy, reducing forecast errors by up to 30%. 
2.1 
Generalizing the Crude Oil Vessel’s Production with Taxonomies 
Representing the Crude Oil vessel taxonomy as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) is cru-
cial for accurately modelling the hierarchical and interdependent relationships between 
various components and processes involved in vessel production. A DAG allows for 
the clear depiction of dependencies where each node represents a speciﬁc component or 
task, and edges indicate the direction of dependency. This structure ensures that complex 
workﬂows are efﬁciently managed, preventing circular dependencies, and facilitating the 
optimization of production schedules, resource allocation, and risk management. More-
over, by representing the taxonomy in a DAG, advanced algorithms like Graph Attention 
Networks (GATs) can be effectively applied to prioritize critical paths, optimize Work 
Breakdown Structures (WBS), and minimize CO2 emissions throughout the supply chain 
(Fig. 1). 
2.2 
Crude Oil Vessel WBS to Graph Conversion 
The methodological approach harnesses graph theory’s conceptual depth to represent the 
WBS of Crude Oil Vessel and integrates robust statistical techniques to ascertain Carbon 
Footprint, Lead Time, Cost Saving related to the major providers for each component 
[4].

2.3 
Graphical Representation of WBS 
To fathom the intricate manufacturing hierarchy within the WBS, a Directed Acyclic 
Graph (DAG) is constructed. Herein: 
Nodes: Denote manufacturing components. For instance, ‘AntiCorrosion-
Coating’ represents a node. 
Edges: These directed pathways represent the information among component depen-
dencies, capturing the essence of Engineer-to-order production system. They can be: 
Assembly, Machinery, Fabrication, Buy (orSell), Test. 
3 
Combined Algorithm Strategy 
3.1 
Graph Attention Networks (GATs) 
GATs, introduced by Velickovic et al. (2018), represent a signiﬁcant advancement in the 
application of attention mechanisms to graph-structured data [1]. Unlike traditional graph 
neural networks, GATs can dynamically assign different attention weights to nodes, 
allowing the model to focus on the most relevant parts of the graph. This capability is 
particularly useful in supply chain management, where certain components or processes 
may have a disproportionately large impact on overall efﬁciency and emissions [2].
5:
Adaptive Neural Network for CO2 Reduction
333
3.2 
Monte Carlo Markov Chain (MCMC) 
MCMC methods have been widely used in supply chain optimization to simulate various 
scenarios and evaluate probabilistic outcomes. The strength of MCMC lies in its ability to 
handle uncertainty and variability, making it an ideal complement to GATs in modeling 
complex supply chains like those involved in crude oil vessel production [3]. 
3.3 
Varied GAT Approach and MCMC 
Recent advancements in deep learning, as detailed in repositories like LabML’s Anno-
tated Deep Learning Paper Implementations, have explored the integration of GATs with 
other probabilistic models [4]. This paper extends these insights by combining a var-
ied GAT approach with MCMC to optimize WBS conﬁgurations, speciﬁcally targeting 
the reduction of CO2 emissions, lead times, and costs in crude oil vessel production. 
This combination has shown potential in supply chain optimization but requires rigorous 
testing to conﬁrm its effectiveness in real-world applications. 
A synthetic dataset of 15,000 companies involved in crude oil vessel production 
in Europe was generated for this study. Each company is represented by its unique 
identiﬁer, a set of activities, and associated metrics, including CO2 emissions, lead time, 
cost savings, and ﬁnancial health [5] (Fig. 2). 
Fig. 2. Data Structure for Score calculation 
The varied GAT approach enhances traditional GATs by introducing variability in 
the attention mechanisms based on real-time data and external factors such as supplier 
reliability and environmental impact [6]. Each node in the WBS graph represents a

dencies. The GAT assigns dynamic attention weights to these nodes, prioritizing those 
with the most signiﬁcant impact on the overall supply chain.

3.4 
MCMC Integration 
The MCMC method is used to simulate various scenarios within the supply chain, 
accounting for uncertainties such as ﬂuctuating material availability or unexpected 
delays. The varied GAT outputs are fed into the MCMC simulations to generate a distri-
bution of possible outcomes. The goal is to identify WBS conﬁgurations that minimize 
CO2 emissions, lead time, and costs.

By employing Markov decision processes in conjunction with these probabilistic 
emission estimates, we optimize supply chain operations. These estimates inform deci-
sions related to transportation routes, sourcing strategies, and inventory management, 
enabling the reduction of carbon emissions while maintaining operational efﬁciency. This 
integrated approach not only addresses environmental sustainability but also enhances 
the overall resilience and sustainability of the Oil & Gas supply chain [7]. 
3.5 
Combined GAT-MCMC Algorithm 
The combined GAT-MCMC algorithm iteratively reﬁnes the WBS conﬁgurations by 
adjusting the attention weights in response to the MCMC outputs [8]. This dynamic 
process ensures that the model continually adapts to new information, optimizing the 
supply chain in real-time (Fig. 3).

4 
Simulation and Result 
The GAT-MCMC model execution on the 15000 records produced an optimistic output in 
optimizing Work Breakdown Structures (WBS) within crude oil vessel production. The 
model achieved a 22% reduction in CO2 emissions, lowering emissions from 500 metric 
tons to 390 metric tons per vessel, consistent with recent studies employing advanced 
optimization techniques [9]. The model also decreased lead times by 16%, bringing aver-
age production times from 24 months down to 20.2 months, corroborating results seen in 
similar GAT-based applications. Additionally, the model increased cost savings by 15%, 
equivalent to an average of $5 million per vessel. This was accomplished by integrating 
cost-related data into the GAT-MCMC framework, optimizing resource allocation and 
supplier selection through MCMC simulations. Finally, the incorporation of ﬁnancial 
health metrics into the model ensured that operational efﬁciency improvements trans-
lated into better ﬁnancial performance, with reduced risks and enhanced proﬁtability. 
Tests on the Financial health scores improved by 9%, with the average score increasing 
from 7.2 to 7.85. These results underscore the model’s robust capability in achieving 
multidimensional optimization within supply chain management (Fig. 4). 
5 
Conclusion and Future Developments 
The integration of varied GAT with MCMC within this study presents a novel and robust 
framework for optimizing complex supply chains, particularly in high-impact sectors 
such as oil and gas. By effectively handling uncertainties and interdependencies, the 
model not only advances the optimization of Work Breakdown Structures (WBS) for 
crude oil vessel production but also offers broader applicability across industries like 
manufacturing, logistics, and energy production. This research highlights the impor-
tance of combining deterministic deep learning models with probabilistic simulations to 
achieve comprehensive and adaptable solutions. The ﬁndings underscore the potential 
for this approach to transform supply chain optimization by addressing both environmen-
tal and ﬁnancial objectives, paving the way for more sustainable and efﬁcient industry 
practices.
